notes: Religion, Mythology & Philosophy draft — 15 clues grounded in Wikipedia lead extracts. Targets thin subcategories: Religious Texts & Terms (adds 3), Symbols/Rituals/Holidays (adds 1), Roman & Norse Mythology (adds 2), Philosophical Schools (adds 1), Philosophers (adds 2), World Religions (adds 2), Bible (adds 1), Greek Mythology (adds 1). Tier spread: $200×3, $400×3, $600×3, $800×3, $1000×3.

questions.clue: Described in Genesis 2–3 as the first home of humanity and fed by four rivers, this biblical paradise is placed by various traditions at the head of the Persian Gulf or somewhere in ancient Mesopotamia.

citations.title: Garden of Eden
citations.snippet: The Garden of Eden, also called Garden of God or the (Terrestrial) Paradise, is the biblical paradise described in Genesis 2–3 and Ezekiel 28 and 31.

questions.clue: Pride, envy, wrath, gluttony, lust, sloth, and greed compose this Catholic grouping, first systematized by Tertullian and Evagrius Ponticus from a mix of Greco-Roman and Biblical antecedents.

citations.title: Seven deadly sins
citations.snippet: The seven deadly sins function as a grouping of major vices within the teachings of Christianity. They are not explicitly listed in the Bible but developed within early Christian theological tradition, drawing on various biblical passages.

questions.clue: Born in Skopje—then part of the Ottoman Empire—to an Albanian family, this Catholic nun founded the Missionaries of Charity in India and was canonized by the Catholic Church in September 2016.

citations.title: Mother Teresa
citations.snippet: Anjezë Gonxhe Bojaxhiu, better known as Mother Teresa or Saint Mother Teresa of Calcutta, was an Albanian-Indian Catholic nun, founder of the Missionaries of Charity and a Catholic saint.

questions.clue: Preserved through named chains of transmitters called isnads, this Arabic body of Islamic tradition records the reported words, actions, and tacit approvals of the Prophet; ranked just below scripture in authority, it serves as the primary source of Sharia rules.

citations.title: Hadith
citations.snippet: Hadith is the Arabic word for a 'report' or an 'account [of an event]' and refers to the Islamic oral tradition of anecdotes containing the purported words, actions, and the silent approvals of the Islamic prophet Muhammad or his immediate

questions.clue: Transmitted orally for centuries before being committed to writing and classified by tradition as śruti—'that which was heard'—these ancient Indian scriptures form the oldest stratum of religious literature on the subcontinent and the primary source of the concept of dharma.

citations.title: Hinduism
citations.snippet: Hinduism is an umbrella term for a range of Indian religious and spiritual traditions (sampradayas) that are unified by adherence to the concept of dharma, a cosmic order maintained by its followers through rituals and righteous living, as

questions.clue: Originally built in ancient Rome to house courts and public functions, this building type—characterized by a central nave flanked by side aisles and an apse at one end—became the standard architectural model for Christian houses of worship after Emperor Constantine embraced Christianity in the 4th century.

citations.title: Basilica
citations.snippet: In Ancient Roman architecture, a basilica was a large public building with multiple functions that was typically built alongside the town's forum. The basilica was in the Latin West equivalent to a stoa in the Greek East. The building gave

questions.clue: The mother of the beloved god Baldr and the foremost goddess of the Norse Æsir, this deity is named in the Prose Edda as Odin's consort and the queen of the Norse divine family.

citations.title: Odin
citations.snippet: Odin is a widely revered god in Norse mythology and Germanic paganism. Most surviving information on Odin comes from Norse mythology, but he figures prominently in the recorded history of Northern Europe. This includes the Roman Empire's pa

questions.clue: Trained as a physician but drawn to philosophy, this Harvard professor was the first educator in the United States to offer a psychology course; his brother Henry was a celebrated American novelist.

citations.title: William James
citations.snippet: William James was an American philosopher and psychologist. The first educator to offer a psychology course in the United States, he is considered to be one of the leading thinkers of the late 19th century, one of the most influential philo

questions.clue: In the religious traditions of the Indian subcontinent, this Sanskrit concept names the endless round of death and rebirth that conditions all sentient existence; liberation from its cycle is held to be the supreme goal of spiritual life.

citations.title: Hinduism
citations.snippet: Hinduism is an umbrella term for a range of Indian religious and spiritual traditions (sampradayas) that are unified by adherence to the concept of dharma, a cosmic order maintained by its followers through rituals and righteous living, as

questions.clue: The son of Laërtes and Anticlea, this king of Ithaca was honored by Homer for his mêtis—his cunning intelligence—as much as for his decade-long struggle to return home after the Trojan War.

citations.title: Odysseus
citations.snippet: In Greek and Roman mythology, Odysseus, also known by the Latin variant Ulysses, is a legendary Greek king of Ithaca and the hero of Homer's epic poem, the Odyssey. Odysseus also plays a key role in Homer's Iliad and other works in that sam

questions.clue: Including texts written between roughly 200 BC and 100 AD, this collection takes its name from a Greek word meaning 'hidden'; while Catholic and Orthodox Bibles incorporate many of them into the Old Testament, most Protestant Bibles place fourteen in a distinct intertestamental section.

citations.title: Biblical apocrypha
citations.snippet: The Biblical apocrypha denotes the collection of ancient books, some of which are believed by some to be of doubtful origin, thought to have been written some time between 200 BC and 100 AD.

questions.clue: Known as the 'sage of Chelsea', this 19th-century Scottish essayist and philosopher attended the University of Edinburgh, first gained renown writing about German Romanticism, and went on to shape the intellectual culture of the Victorian era.

citations.title: Thomas Carlyle
citations.snippet: Thomas Carlyle was a Scottish essayist, historian and philosopher. Known as the "sage of Chelsea, London", his writings strongly influenced the intellectual and artistic culture of the Victorian era.

questions.clue: Defined largely through Albert Camus's 1942 essay 'The Myth of Sisyphus', this philosophical school holds that humans instinctively seek meaning in a universe that offers none—a tension Camus considered the defining predicament of human existence.

citations.title: Albert Camus
citations.snippet: Albert Camus was a French philosopher, novelist, author, dramatist, journalist, world federalist, and political activist. He was the recipient of the 1957 Nobel Prize in Literature at the age of 44, the second-youngest recipient in history,

questions.clue: Among the four Puruṣārthas—the canonical goals of human life in Indian religious thought—this concept stands alone at the apex, above wealth, pleasure, and moral duty; it names the irreversible liberation of the self from the cycle of rebirth.

questions.clue: The monstrous wolf destined in Norse myth to slay Odin at Ragnarök, this creature fulfills its doom at the final battle before being killed by the Allfather's son Víðarr.
